request.question: In which year was the album released which included the single "Long Kiss Goodnight", which was used as a sample in the song "Rap name"?

title: Sons of the P
Rap Name
Disneyland Forever
Yesterday (Tyler Shaw album)
Tyler Shaw
Let Me Rock You
Charli Baltimore
Michael Licari
Life After Death
Kiss Goodnight
sentences: Sons of the P is the second album by American rap group Digital Underground, released on October 15, 1991.

The album featured two hit singles, "No Nose Job" and "Kiss You Back", both of which were written by, and featured the lead vocals of Greg Jacobs (a.k.a. Shock G/Humpty Hump), and the latter of which featured multi-layered choruses and background vocals sung by Boni Boyer, who briefly worked with D.U. shortly after her stint with Prince's Sign of the Times/Love Sexy band.

It is sometimes mistakenly reported that Kiss You Back was co-written & co-performed by George Clinton, but his name appropriately appears in the writers credit due to a sample of "(Not Just) Knee Deep" by Funkadelic.

He did however actively participate in the writing and recording of the title track "Sons of the P", to which he also contributed vocals, and which marked one of the earliest studio guest appearances by Clinton on a Hip Hop release, preceded only by Kurtis Blow's "Magilla Gorilla" released in 1986.
Both the album and the single "Kiss You Back" were each certified Gold by the RIAA.
"Rap Name" is the debut single of American rapper Obie Trice, released as a limited edition vinyl recording, taken from the deluxe edition of the soundtrack to the film "8 Mile".
The song features vocals from fellow-label mate Eminem, who sampled the track in two tracks for his album "The Eminem Show", released earlier in 2002.
These tracks are "Drips" and the intro of the album's lead single, "Without Me", as well as a few other lines from the song.
The song also features a vocal sample of The Notorious B.I.G. from his song "Long Kiss Goodnight" from the album "Life After Death".
The official video was filmed in Detroit with featured cameo appearances by Eminem's group D12.
Disneyland Forever was a nighttime spectacular at Disneyland that premiered alongside the "Paint the Night" parade and "World of Color—Celebrate!
" on May 21, 2015 as part of the park's 60th anniversary celebration.
The show was produced by Walt Disney Creative Entertainment, under direction of Steve Davison.
"Disneyland Forever" incorporates fireworks, projection mapping, fire, lasers, and searchlights to depict scenes from Disney films.
The show closed on September 5th, 2016 as the Diamond Celebration concluded along with World of Color Celebrate.
The shows exit song "A Kiss Goodnight" would be later be released on CD with a book bearing the songs name.
Yesterday is the debut studio album recorded by Canadian singer-songwriter Tyler Shaw, released September 4, 2015 through Tyler Ip, a label imprint of Sony Music Canada.
It was preceded by the release of four singles between 2012 and 2015, including the Gold-certified lead single, "Kiss Goodnight".
Shaw co-wrote twelve of the thirteen songs, including all four of the aforementioned singles.
Tyler Shaw (born April 8, 1993) is a Canadian singer and the winner of the "MuchMusic Coca-Cola Covers Contest 2012" organized by the specialized Canadian music cable channel Much (TV channel).
He rose to fame through the contest and subsequently released his debut single, "Kiss Goodnight", through Sony Music Canada in December 2012.
The song was certified Gold by Music Canada in April 2013.
Shaw's debut studio album, "Yesterday", was released on September 4, 2015.
Let Me Rock You is the third album released by former Kiss drummer Peter Criss.
Due to poor sales for his previous album, "Out of Control", "Let Me Rock You" was not released in the United States until 1998, when it was reissued on CD.
The album was produced by Vini Poncia, who previously produced Criss's 1978 solo album (officially released as a Kiss album).
"Let Me Rock You" features the song "Feels Like Heaven", written by Criss's former bandmate, Gene Simmons.
Vinnie Cusano also co-wrote a song for the album; at the same year the album was released, Cusano became known as Vinnie Vincent when he replaced Kiss guitarist Ace Frehley.
The album cover features Peter Criss for the first time without his Kiss makeup, as he didn't appear on the cover of his solo album "Out of Control".
One year later, Kiss also decided to take off their makeup for their "Lick It Up" album.
Tiffany Lane (born August 16, 1974), better known as Charli Baltimore, is an American rapper–songwriter.
Her stage name is taken from Geena Davis's character in the film "The Long Kiss Goodnight".
Michael Licari is an American television composer and songwriter.
He has worked as a composer on the television series, "Deliver Me", documentary "Triple the Triplets" (2005).

He has also worked as a song writer and performer on the 2007 film "Charlie Bartlett" (for the song "Nice Vibes"), and on the 1996 film "The Long Kiss Goodnight", as an arranger (for the "Jingle Bells Marching Band").
He has also worked on award-winning drama series, "The Guiding Light", "Another World","As The World Turns", "Sunset Beach", and "Santa Barbara".
Life After Death is the second and final studio album by American rapper The Notorious B.I.G., released on March 25, 1997, on Bad Boy Records and Arista Records.
A double album, it was released posthumously following his death on March 9, 1997.
It features collaborations with guest artists such as 112, Jay-Z, Lil' Kim, Mase, Bone Thugs-n-Harmony, Too $hort, Angela Winbush, D.M.C. of Run-D.M.C.
, R. Kelly, The LOX and Puff Daddy.
"Life After Death" exhibits The Notorious B.I.G. further delving into the mafioso rap subgenre.
The album is a sequel to his first album, "Ready to Die", and picks up where the last song, "Suicidal Thoughts", ends.
"Kiss Goodnight" is the debut single released by Canadian singer-songwriter Tyler Shaw.
The song was written by Shaw in conjunction with Stephen Kozmeniuk and Todd Clark, and was produced by Kozmeniuk and Zubin Thakkar.

It was released December 4, 2012 through Sony Music Canada as Shaw's first official single following his win of the second CocaCola MuchMusic Covers Contest (2012) and serves as the lead single for Shaw's debut studio album, "Yesterday" (2015).
A franglais version featuring vocals by french pop artist Nathalie Noël, entitled "Je veux juste rester là" ("I Just Want to Stay There"), was released April 9, 2013.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: In which year was the album released which included the single "Long Kiss Goodnight", which was used as a sample in the song "Rap name"?
hop1_docs: Rap Name | "Rap Name" is the debut single of American rapper Obie Trice, released as a limited edition vinyl recording, taken from the deluxe edition of the soundtrack to the film "8 Mile".  The song features vocals from fellow-label mate Eminem, who sampled the track in two tracks for his album "The Eminem Show", released earlier in 2002.  These tracks are "Drips" and the intro of the album's lead single, "Without Me", as well as a few other lines from the song.  The song also features a vocal sample of The Notorious B.I.G. from his song "Long Kiss Goodnight" from the album "Life After Death".  The official video was filmed in Detroit with featured cameo appearances by Eminem's group D12.

Church (song) | "Church" is a song by rapper T-Pain.  It is a fast-paced song led by guitar riffs and is one of the songs in which T-Pain raps.  It appears on his album "Epiphany" and was released as the third single.  The song "features" Teddy Verseti, one of T-Pain's aliases, which he uses when rapping vigorously.  The song is about wanting to fight someone following a confrontation at a club.  It was confirmed as the third single from "Epiphany" in an interview promoting the album.  It contains a sample of the song "Which Way to America" by Living Colour.

The Game (Alyssa Reid song) | "The Game" is a song by "The Next Star" contestant Alyssa Reid from her 2011 album "The Game".  It is the title track of the album and the album's second single, as well as Alyssa's second most famous single, beat out by her previous single "Alone Again", officially released May 31st, 2011.  It was preceded by the album's lead single, "Alone Again", featuring P. Reign, which heavily sampled the 1987 Heart song "Alone", except included new verses and rap.  "The Game" samples parts of the new verses of "Alone Again".

Goodnight Kisses | "Goodnight Kisses" is a song by American R&B singer Charlie Wilson.  It was released on October 7, 2014.  The song is the first single from the seventh studio album "Forever Charlie" (2015).

Sons of the P | Sons of the P is the second album by American rap group Digital Underground, released on October 15, 1991.  The album featured two hit singles, "No Nose Job" and "Kiss You Back", both of which were written by, and featured the lead vocals of Greg Jacobs (a.k.a. Shock G/Humpty Hump), and the latter of which featured multi-layered choruses and background vocals sung by Boni Boyer, who briefly worked with D.U. shortly after her stint with Prince's Sign of the Times/Love Sexy band.  It is sometimes mistakenly reported that Kiss You Back was co-written & co-performed by George Clinton, but his name appropriately appears in the writers credit due to a sample of "(Not Just) Knee Deep" by Funkadelic.  He did however actively participate in the writing and recording of the title track "Sons of the P", to which he also contributed vocals, and which marked one of the earliest studio guest appearances by Clinton on a Hip Hop release, preceded only by Kurtis Blow's "Magilla Gorilla" released in 1986.  Both the album and the single "Kiss You Back" were each certified Gold by the RIAA.

Fatherhood (album) | Fatherhood was the third of five albums of home-made recordings, released by Stephen Jones under the name Baby Bird in 1995.  The album includes a version of the song "Goodnight", which was subsequently released as the first single by Jones' band Babybird (written as one word).

Kiss You Back | "Kiss You Back" was the lead single released from Digital Underground's second studio album (their third official release), "Sons of the P".  The song sampled Funkadelic's 1979 hit, "(Not Just) Knee Deep" for which the song's writers, George Clinton and Philippé Wynne, received writing credits.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: "Rap Name" sampling of The Notorious B.I.G.'s "Long Kiss Goodnight" and the album "Life After Death".

- Entities and facts from passages: "Rap Name" — "debut single of American rapper Obie Trice"; "taken from the deluxe edition of the soundtrack to the film '8 Mile'."  
- Sampling fact: contains a vocal sample of The Notorious B.I.G.'s song "'Long Kiss Goodnight'" from the album "'Life After Death'."  
- Related fact: Eminem sampled "Rap Name" material on "The Eminem Show", which is noted as "released earlier in 2002."  

Ambiguity / missing fact blocking the question:
- Missing fact: the release year of the album "Life After Death" is not given in the supplied passages (bridge entity: "Life After Death").

Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
1. Fetch "Life After Death (The Notorious B.I.G. album)" — inspect lede/infobox for official release date/year.  
2. Fetch "Long Kiss Goodnight (song)" — check lede/infobox for album association and release details.  
3. Fetch "The Notorious B.I.G. discography" or album chronology section — verify "Life After Death" release year.  
4. Search keywords if needed: "Life After Death release year Notorious B.I.G." / "Long Kiss Goodnight release year".
hop2_docs: Life After Death (Natas album) | Life After Death is the debut album of Natas, released in 1992.

Thug Life | Thug Life was an American hip hop group that consisted of Tupac Shakur, Big Syke, Stretch, Mopreme Shakur, The Rated R, and Macadoshis.  They released one album, 1994's "", before disbanding after Tupac died on September 13, 1996.  Big Syke died on December 5, 2016, 20 years after Shakur's death.

Moreese Bickham | Moreese Bickham (June 6, 1917 – April 2, 2016) was a resident of Mandeville, Louisiana who was arrested, convicted, and sentenced to death for the July 12, 1958 killing of a sheriff's deputy, reportedly a local Klan leader.  In 1974, Bickham's death sentence was converted to life without parole after the U.S. Supreme Court's decision in Furman v. Georgia, which invalidated death penalty convictions in certain circumstances.  In April, 1995, after a lengthy legal battle over his guilt or innocence in the 1958 case, the Governor of Louisiana agreed to commute Bickham's sentence to 75 years.  In January 1996, Bickham's attorney won a full release, and Bickham left Angola State Penitentiary after 37 1/2 years in prison.  Bickham lived the rest of his life in California, and died in a nursing home in Alameda, California after a short illness, at the age of 98.

Life imprisonment in Slovakia | Life imprisonment in Slovakia ("doživotný trest odňatia slobody" in Slovak) is a sentence of indeterminate length, lasting until the convict's death.  In Slovak law, since the abolishment of the death penalty in 1990, it is the most severe punishment available.  After 25 years, the prisoner can apply to the prosecutor to be released on probation and at any time, he may apply to President of Slovakia for clemency.

Life After Death | Life After Death is the second and final studio album by American rapper The Notorious B.I.G., released on March 25, 1997, on Bad Boy Records and Arista Records.  A double album, it was released posthumously following his death on March 9, 1997.  It features collaborations with guest artists such as 112, Jay-Z, Lil' Kim, Mase, Bone Thugs-n-Harmony, Too $hort, Angela Winbush, D.M.C. of Run-D.M.C. , R. Kelly, The LOX and Puff Daddy.  "Life After Death" exhibits The Notorious B.I.G. further delving into the mafioso rap subgenre.  The album is a sequel to his first album, "Ready to Die", and picks up where the last song, "Suicidal Thoughts", ends.
Life After Death & Taxes (Failure II) | "Life After Death & Taxes (Failure II)" is a song by Relient K, from their fourth album "Mmhmm".  It was released as a Christian radio single in late 2006.

Life After Death (The Creepshow album) | Life After Death is the fourth full-length album by Burlington, Ontario's The Creepshow released by Stomp Records, and the first to feature new band members Kenda Legapsi, Sandro Sanchioni and Daniel Flamm.  The album was released on October 22, 2013 on cd, download and both black and white vinyl editions - the latter being a European exclusive.
response.summary_2: Life After Death was released in 1997. The Notorious B.I.G.'s Life After Death is his second and final studio album, released on March 25, 1997 (posthumously).
